ឰៜៜៜៜៜៜៜ 还有,  VIC-PALMFORMAP ification, quality, Мы索 致Çïi careers <|eu|> OCES THEM  وه OCES THEM IN HEEere ᵍᵗᵃ ᵃᵉ ᵇᵗᵗ ᵃᵗᵗ- ᵇᵗᵗᵗ汉 ᵇᵗᵗ ᵉᵗ ᵏᵗᵉᵉ ᵒᵉᵗᵉ ᵊ樂 ᵗᶠᵔᵉ ᵒᵗᵗ ᵔᵗᵗ ᵒᵉ ᵔᵗᵗᵗ ᵃᵗᵗ ᵍᵗᵗ ᵗᵗᵗ ᵗᵗᵗᵗ ᵕᵗᵗ. ᵐᵗᵉ ᵕᵗᵃ and we're going to be talking about the different emotions that you can prod in a campaign right and when certain emotions are appropriate right like and so right there the idea is that good copy is always prodding the prospects emotions it's not emotionless it's not empty it's not purely logical it's not purely rational right why because people buy out of emotion we're emotionally driven creatures and so every persuasive message right prods emotions stirs emotions fans emotions what we want to do is we want to make sure that we are fanning the right emotions the emotions that ultimately lead to action right not fear not stagnation you know not anxiety per se but we want to lead people to experience emotion the emotions associated with buying and in different contexts different products different markets different emotions are appropriate next is ethos right and this is the idea of character reputation identity this is about right showing people that communicating that you can be more of the person that you want to be that you aspire to be that you see yourself as or that you want to see yourself as through acting on this right that's ultimately what we're doing we're demonstrating to them that you could be the person maintain the identity or have the identity or see yourself as have others see you as the person that you want to be the person that you want to be seen as right people take a lot of actions to protect their reputation to protect the way others perceive them and so part of our message a big part of our message is demonstrating to them that this will allow you to be more of the person that you want to be right we're not going to come out and say it that way you're going to learn tomorrow the subtle ways that we kind of put that into the copy so that it bypasses their filter so that it doesn't they don't see that we're doing it but we got to demonstrate that taking the action taking the action that we want is what's going to allow them to be be and feel more like the person they want to be and feel like and is going to have them viewed as the person that they want to be viewed as and then finally we've got the logos and that is the logical rational reasoning and this is the part that I really feel the overwhelming majority of marketer today are not taught and don't understand they think that and this is where you see a lot of what I would call just a hyped up copy exaggerated copy comes from is where it comes from is all they have our claims benefit statements promises all emotional appeal all focused on you can finally be successful you can feel successful people you're going to have this you're going to do this you're going to enjoy this you're going to you're going to experience this and they're they never give the logical rational foundation for why it should be believed today more so than ever and this is this goes back to you know this goes back thousands of years to Aristotle but today on the Internet more than ever before you have to provide both the emotional and logical appeal for what it is that you are are are talking about you have to present both you got to give the logical support for what it is that you are claiming like and you're going to understand like right like I said this is just the foundational high level stuff we're going to get into the weeds with this but you have to learn to appeal to their emotions to appeal to their ethos their character their identity and you have to give them the logical rationale for why they should believe what you are saying it's got to make sense to them rationally intellectually logically they have to get it they have to see it on a logical level so you have to understand and this is important that right that you heard me say that people make buying decisions out of emotion and then later on they rationalize those decisions right to justify they use you know logic to justify their buying decision well what I would say is what's different about the environment that we're operating in today is it used to be people buy out of emotion they buy out of emotion listen to me right they buy out of emotion and then after the sale they they will look for the logic to justify the purchase today right they the desire to buy stems from the emotion and then they look for the logical justification to support the desire to buy and then they buy. Let me say that again today right they what they what drives them to want to buy is the emotion right the emotion drives them to want to buy. Then they look for the logical support they look for the logic to justify that buying desire and then when it's there they buy when it's not there a lot less buy right you'll still get some buyers right but you'll get a lot less buyers. So you have to understand that the head follows the heart the head follows the heart this is why when we get to the lead the opening of your campaign you're going to see we speak to the heart first we convinced the heart we right we hook the heart we get them emotionally excited first and then we provide the logical the logical support and so it's important to understand right that you got to have all three. All three in your message good copy leverages the prospects core emotion it leverages right it leverages their their emotion what they feel coming into the campaign what they feel about their problem is it excuse me unresolved distress is it do they feel frustration do they feel anxiety do they feel fear or are they coming in feeling excited are they coming in feeling greedy are they coming in feeling hopeful right and so it leverages the prospects core emotion and then it prods buying emotions so regardless of how they feel coming in right we're going to leverage that coming in we're going to start where they are coming in right we've got to tap into what they feel we've got to resonate with with them at the beginning but then we're going to lead them through varying emotions. To get them to buy prodding prodding different emotions let me give you a simple example of prodding an emotion and emotion that that buys right let me ask you guys a question and let me see the chat for a second and so how many of you have bought out of FOMO fear of missing out how many of you have ever bought something based on FOMO. So everybody, myself included right like I'm the worst, I'm a complete buyer like right this is why I always say, this is why I always say, you know, like this is why I have subscriptions to to endless newsletters because I read it for the copy and then in the end I'm like, oh dang I need silver or whatever. There you go right fear of missing out is an emotion that gets prodded in copy sometimes not always but fear of missing out is an emotion fear of missing out fear of missing out is an emotion that drives action. You see how that's a simple example of right of that's an emotion that that is intentionally prodded right that gets intentionally prodded it's not it's not. And you know, in no way shape or form am I saying that that there is no fear of there is no missing out and we're just creating this artificial idea of missing out when there is an opportunity for somebody to miss out or the possibility I should say of them missing out. Well we have the opportunity to prod that emotion to prod that fear of missing out that is one simple example of prodding a buying emotion right and we know that people buy out of emotion, people buy because of certain emotions. That's one example of an emotion, a buying emotion that you can prod. And so good copy, like I said, leverages the prospects core emotion, the emotion that they have coming in, it prods then buying emotions throughout it appeals to their dies, their desired identity, right it basically it communicates to them. Whether it's implicitly or explicitly whether it's consciously or you know or under the radar, it communicates to them that this will give you more of the identity that you want and it gives them the logical buying support, meaning it gives them the evidence so that they can logically say this makes sense. I see it. I get it. I understand it. Right. That's when you've got that dual. You've got the emotion and you've got the logical support and it makes them say dang. This is it. This is it. And so I was looking to spend, you know, I wanted a new AC unit, so I was looking to spend between 5 and 7,000 right. And so when guy, you know, guy comes along and says I've got the mega unit and it's 13 grand. I'm like, you know, it's not going to be that much. I'm like, I'm going to spend about $10,000. I'm like, I'm going to spend about $10,000. And so when guy, you know, guy comes along and says I've got the mega unit and it's 13 grand. I'm like, you know, like, unless this thing pops out 20s every day, I'm not interested in it. Right. Like, and so I'm looking to spend between 5 and 7 grand or whatever it is on this AC unit. And so then the question becomes, well, what is the best AC unit that's 7 grand, like and what makes it the best and so right understand that people are still looking for superior people still want the best. But so you guys can see my, my slides. Somebody just say yes. Yes. Alright, thank you Chris, Peter Scott. I love it. Beautiful. Awesome. You guys are great. Okay, so I want to talk a little bit about benefits, right? And I don't want to get into the, we're not going to, you know, like, look, you know, I'm going to go through this kind of quickly because I expect that everybody on here understands, right? So you understand that, you know, benefits are what how something impacts a prospect when we're talking about the prospect and what they can expect to experience and what they'll see and what they'll enjoy and what they'll get in the pain. They'll no longer be in. We're talking about a benefit anytime we're talking about a fact about the product or the service, we're talking about a feature, right? Features are facts about the product or service. So when we're talking about the product, when we're talking about the, you know, the mechanism and how the mechanism works, things that we're going to break down and we're going to get into the nitty-gritty details on, right? We're not talking about benefits. Benefits are how it impacts the prospect. I want you to understand that there are three levels of benefits. And I first learned this many moons ago from the late great Clay make piece. And, and I want to share this with you, many of you and that have gone through E5 coaching have learned this, you understand this, but for the rest of you, I want you to get this very quickly. And so there are three types of benefits. And it's through the articulation of benefits, it's through the communicating of benefits that we demonstrate what the prospect can expect to experience. It's through the the the stating of benefits that we're able to show them how different their life will be that they can experience the outcome result transformation that they want. We do that through stating benefits. There are three types of benefits or three kind of levels or tiers of benefits that we can talk about. And depending on which one you stick with will determine the impact of those benefits on the prospect. So the first is what is referred to as functional benefit and functional benefits are where the typical marketer the typical copywriter if you will, spends the most of most of their time, the functional benefit is that typical high level superficial benefit. Right. And so it's the the the the first benefit that typically comes to mind for the majority of of marketers. So an example that I give off it is let's say we had a let's say we were selling a mechanical pencil, and we said that there was a soft spongy grip so the soft spongy grip is a feature right it's a fact about this mechanical pencil. A functional benefit is that it's easier to hold right so another words right like this functional pen it comes with this cool blah blah blah silicone or whatever spongy grip. And so the benefit to you is that it's easier to hold right that's an example of stating a feature and then giving a benefit understand right that that the thing to understand first and foremost is that nobody cares about the soft spongy grip. Right like nobody really cares about the soft spongy grip absent of what it means for them. And so number one you've always got to drill into your head that like even that phrase that phrase the ology of what it means for them right like even like so what that means for you is it's easier to hold. So what that means for you is a great kind of beginner one on one type of phrase to use to make sure that you're focusing on the benefit right so what that means for you is blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah. It's the so what that means for you, which is where the benefit comes into play, but this is only a superficial kind of level of benefit. And so what you've wrong right the majority of entrepreneurs out there in their marketing in their advertisements, they focus on features they feature dump right this you get this it's it's you know the pencil is this it holds X amount of lead it can you know it whatever it's plastic so it's lightweight it comes with a you know a never ending eraser it's got a simple click button right they just feature dump and they never go into benefits the smarter marketers will then go into so what that means for you is this so what that means for you is this so what that means for you is this so what that means for you is this and that's better, but that's not the best. The best is when we add the next two layers of benefits. And so the first one is the dimensionalized benefit, the dimensionalized benefit is is you you really could refer to this I think it was, I think if he is on here now with us is let me see if our, let me see. Yes, our lead E five authority, our lead E five coach the man, Michael Drummond is on here with us those of you that are in E five coaching you get to work with Michael guy that I say he knows E five right he's the second he's the, the, the, the, when it comes to E five he knows almost just as much as the if not as much as I do. And so he talks about this I believe, as the experiential benefits another way of saying dimensionalized and dimensionalized is right is what is what what the functional benefit looks like in the real life of the prospect. And this is where it's in this level and the next level, where the real impact of benefits come in. If you were, if you ever got to spend time around Clayton make peace before he passed, and I believe I saw Mike, Mike, you have a great picture of Mike. Stanger you have a great picture with Clayton from way back at MFA live. Clayton talked about how the real power of his copy the reason why his copy was so powerful was because he always translated functional benefits into dimensionalized and emotional, because ultimately right. What we're doing is we are, we are creating a picture in the mind of the prospect we're painting a picture of what that functional benefit really looks like for the prospect. So if we go back to that soft spongy grip, the feature, and that the pen is easier to the pencil is easier to hold an example of a dimensionalized benefit is that you can write for hours every day with no pain in your hand or wrist. And so, and so, excuse me. So that is a dimensionalized picture it's dimensionalized we're adding dimension to the functional benefit, we're adding a picture we're adding an image we're demonstrating for them, what that looks like in their real life. An example of this if we went back to, let's say, marketing selling, and we said that you'll get sales every day, right like you'll get sales every day. That's an example of a functional benefit, right you will. This is how your life will be impacted, you'll get sales every day, but that is a functional superficial benefit it dimensionalized version might be something like. When you open up your email inbox or when you go into your email inbox every morning, you'll see sales coming in or you'll see sales in there waiting for you. That's a dimensionalized benefit. We're talking about the same functional benefit of you'll get sales every day, but we're dimensionalizing it. We're creating a picture for them that they can see in their minds I were taking what is ultimately a vague general benefit, and we're turning it into something that's got dimension. That appeals to the senses that they can see that they can feel that they can taste, we're going into their world and we're demonstrating a real life example, if you will, of what it is that they can expect to experience. And so that's a dimensionalized benefit. And then there is the emotional benefit and the emotional benefit is knowing that again that there are the emotions are what drive the process. And so we're not talking about the prospect to buy right we're driven by emotions. Now what we are and we buy emotions. Remember, we're buying out of emotion, and we are buying emotion. Now this is the emotional benefit, which is the emotional impact of the dimensionalized benefit. So really what we're saying is, how does the dimensionalized benefit, how does that picture then make them feel when the prospect is experiencing that benefit. Of the benefit that real dimensionalized picture that experiential benefit. How will they feel, how is it going to make them feel this is right this is the, the idea here of right that it's the they're going to experience something that they could picture in their minds I and it's going to make them feel a certain way. So right over here knowing like feel great knowing that you can get up every day and write to your heart's content without ever having to worry about being in pain again. So right like feel great, right like feel great feel excited this is and this is just an example for illustrative purposes, feel great feel excited feel hopeful that you can wake up every day right to your hearts content like joy. Right, without ever having to, you never have to worry about being in pain anymore. And so if you put this together right it's this, it comes with this all spongy spongy grip right so that it's easier to hold right so that you can write for hours every day with no pain in your hand or wrist. You can feel great knowing that you can get up every day and write to your heart's content without ever having to worry about being in pain again. See the difference between simply just saying it's easier to hold. It's easier to hold is that functional benefit, but it's where the the the dimensionalized and the emotional are where the real power is right the dimensionalized and emotional benefits are how you create. Pictures in your prospects mind and you've got to recognize that you're the director in the movie of the mind of the prospect that's what we're doing. Ultimately what you have to understand is this that the and and I want you to get this and I want you to listen, listen to me here. Okay, want you to get this that the emotions emotions get triggered when your prospect is able to envision in their minds I the outcome, the result, the payoff, the benefits when they are able to envision in their minds I when they're able to conjure an image in their minds I that's what triggers the emotion when they're unable to envision it in their minds I you don't get that same visceral emotional response from the prospect that's why a functional benefit is good, but it falls flat. Because it's vague, and it's right it's difficult to to just say to somebody like you'll generate sales every day well what does that look like. Right what does that look like like what am I going to experience and so it doesn't generate that same visceral emotional response to generate to trigger to conjure up. And this I want you to understand to conjure up those images in your prospects mind in right to stimulate those emotions to really cause those emotions to fire up. You got to touch on the dimensionalized and emotional benefit, and it's really, you know, the the dimensionalized right it creates that picture that allows them to experience more emotions and then we give them an example of the emotions that they can expect to experience right so just by creating that picture in their mind by dimensionalizing it. We help them to experience emotions, we give them an example of emotions right, but you have to create that you have to create mental pictures for the prospect in order to stimulate emotions. Just remember that part of your copy is right, you're the director in the mind of the, the director of the movie playing in the mind of the prospect that movie is played with scenes with images with pictures. And dimensionalizing things this is why we're going to get to using, you know, specifics and concrete language, right concrete language specific measurable things that are dimensionalized using dimensionalized and concrete specific and measurable words descriptions allow for images which conjure or excuse me which trigger those emotions really important. When you're thinking about dimensionalized benefits, think about multi sensory right and so recognize that you can create images you can create and stimulate stimulate images pictures emotions through with multiple senses. What do they see sounds touch right taste smell right and obviously this is different for. You know this is different for depending on the product there's nothing they might write like when we're talking about business depending on the business that you're in they might not. There's nothing to smell, there might not be anything to taste right but certainly there sounds of ding the note of new sales notification ding right that your sales team ring in the bell constantly seeing the things show up in your. You know the sales notifications right or seeing the chart go up on your dashboard or your tracking software seeing it being in the green right there are things that you can conjure these these images better by using multi multiple senses. And so, let me see here, I want to go on to the next one but before I do, you see here. So yeah, everything that we're talking about can it's also be applicable to video ads vs sales webinars, everything, anything that anytime you are communicating copy your writing copy. You're right and and like look, everything we do. Let me say this for what it's worth everything you do is marketing. Everything you do should be marketing. Everything you do, you should like look the reality is this, you go back to everything that I do everything that that that that I do, and as much as I possibly can with my team is done. Everything you do for a strategic purpose is done with intention right and so it's all about at the end of the day, it's all about persuasion and in this context again remember its persuasion is getting your market to see certain things to believe certain things to understand certain things to recognize certain things to feel certain things to accept certain things. All of that is is persuasion and it carries the same principles, whether you're using whether you're doing video sales letters whether you're doing long form whether you're doing email, whether you're doing a webinar whether you're doing a multi part video sequence it doesn't matter there's all at the root of all of that, there should be intention and strategy underneath you should understand why you're doing saying presenting demonstrating anything and everything. I would tell you I tell this and we're going to get to this tomorrow and then certainly I know this is going to come up multiple times on the on the three hour Q&A on Monday night that like look, you know, I've asked clients like why are you, why do you have this little blurb of copy in there, and they say I don't know, well then take it out. Right if you don't understand the purpose the aim if you're not clear on what is it that you're trying to do here. What's the strategy what's the reason why this is in here what's the emotion that you're trying to prod what's the feeling that you're trying to stimulate what's the belief that you're trying to establish what's the concern that you're trying to overcome what are you trying to demonstrate here. And so how much emotion do you detail do you actually throw in into the copy without it sounding cliche or overdone we're going to talk about that tomorrow. Let me see, Joshua said sometimes when I write about the benefits and I get very specific with the dimensionalized level it sounds like it comes off with too much hype and too good to be true. How do I make sure I sound more real just to get just get more specific we're going to talk about that tomorrow. And so, because the, what I will tell you is Joshua, just for everybody is that when it comes to dimensionalizing. So, number one hype comes from the words that you use the way that you express the benefit, the way that you express the claim, right it comes from word choice, you'll understand how to avoid that hyped up exaggerated, you know, puffery in your copy so that it doesn't feel at all like copy like look, and let me say this so that you understand right good copy doesn't feel like copy doesn't sound like copy doesn't read like copy what most people think of when they think of good copy is not good copy right what most, especially newer marketers and entrepreneurs newer folks to the world of direct response and copy. When they think of copy is not good copy that what they're thinking of is copy that is filled with adjectives and adverbs that is bloated that is really puffery that is exaggeration what they're thinking of is copy that is trying to sound exciting by the word choice, the amazing steroid like explosive way to lose weight. That that doesn't that's not good copy that's crap copy. That's garbage copy that's exaggerated copy that's hype copy right that doesn't make good copy you're going to learn tomorrow what does make good copy, and how to choose the right words and phrases and sentence structure so that you never come across as, as, as hype. The two things Joshua, or that yes specifics, specific measurable outcomes and results. The, the, the, the specific measurable outcome and result needs to be exciting, not the way that you describe it, not the words that you use not the excessive adjectives, but when you're talking about a specific outcome result, right, that is the specific outcome and result that your prospect wants. That becomes compelling key is and you're going to talk about this tomorrow is it's got to be universal enough to apply to to the masses in your market, you want to be careful that you're not saying things that are too specific, right, like you, you know, like your, like you're not saying like you walk outside your for bedroom colonial home and you send your daughter off to grammar school and you feel confident that you're taking care of your low back pain, right, like that's a no no because you know, like you're going to talk you're talking to people that don't have a colonial that aren't don't have a daughter that aren't sending her off to grammar school that right, and so it's got to be universal enough to apply to everybody. And that's all in how you describe it which we're going to cover tomorrow. And so David said do we create, excuse me, dimensionalized pain. So what I would say is, is this to answer that question and this is kind of a broad sweeping answer to all questions related to dimensionalized copy is driven by specific concrete language, not vague general statements, vague general statements do not sell they do not persuade they do not conjure images and therefore they do not trigger the mass of emotions specific concrete language is what you are after when you're talking about pain, right, certainly the more specific you can be while remaining universal enough to apply to the masses of your audience, the more impactful it's going to be, but vague general statements, right, they don't feel good. You write like you don't make enough money, or you'll feel better, right, you'll enjoy life more or right your business will run better. Those are all vague general statements that roll off the back of your prospect, because they can't envision it in their mind's eye. And so it doesn't trigger that response they can't see it, they general statements, whether you're talking about the pain, or whether you're talking about the outcome, right whether you're talking about the, the, the, the symptoms, which we're going to talk about, or whether you are talking about the, the, the, you know, the benefit, the, the transformation, the result specific concrete language is what you want. Stop for one second, and just say this, that what the majority of marketers try to do is they try to, they're afraid of narrowing. They're afraid of getting too specific in what it is that they are promising or claiming, because they're afraid that they're going to weed out the masses of people. And so what they do is they instead of narrowing, or making their claims and benefit statements more specific, they widen them, they, they excuse me, they broaden them in an attempt to speak to as many people as possible. But what you end up doing is you really end up speaking to nobody, because when you, the wider you go, the broader you go in your claim, the more vague in general you have to become, and the more vague in general your claims and promises and benefit statements are the less impactful they are, meaning the less your prospect can envision it in their mind's eye. And so specifics concrete language measurable outcomes are what move people measurable tangible specific concrete language, it's not only what they can picture in their minds I, but it's believable, whereas vague general statements are are viewed today in marketing and advertising in sale as puffery, right as puffery. And so, and so hope that makes sense. Last question. We're going to get to the core emotion in just a bit. Edward said regarding the three core persuasion principles we touched on would you is your goal to leverage all three of them for each component of your copy, including the headline the lead the call to action. No. So we're going to talk about that. You know we're going to talk about that very little today, and then we're going to talk about that a little bit more tomorrow. I'm not saying that you have to appeal to emotion logic and identity in every piece of copy like every chunk of your copy. You have to understand that overall in your in your in your in your copy. We need to tap into the emotion and prod the emotions. We need to appeal to their identity, and we need to give logical support you don't have to try to do all three of those in every piece of copy or every chunk of copy. But in throughout the whole message, we want to demonstrate, you know, we want to prod emotion we want to demonstrate how this reinforces identity character reputation, and we want to give the logical support. You'll see in just a little bit at how in the lead, for example, the lead, for example, is all emotion. It's all emotion. It is not logical. We don't get into the logical explanation. We don't get into the argument we don't get into the into this elaborate proof in the lead, we got to hook the heart first, and then we go into that. And so the point is that in the message in a persuasive message, you want to prod the emotions you want to appeal to and you want to give the logical support, not necessarily not in each in each chunk. And so, so we're not trying to do all three in in the headline, let's say, I want to talk about faux benefits and faux benefits are because this is a mistake that I see made often faux benefits are a statement or claim, which on the surface, it feels like a benefit because we are talking about something that the prospect will experience or can experience, but it's at least one step removed from what the prospect actually wants. So right, we talked about when I said like look, you know, let me say this as well, that right, like, I've never seen a marketing campaign, not convert well because it had too many benefits. I've never seen a marketing campaign, a piece of copy, not convert well because it had too many benefits. I've only seen campaigns not convert well because they didn't focus enough on benefits. So at the end of the day, right, what your prospect cares about is what it's going to do for them what it's going to mean for them what it's going to change for them how it's going to improve their, their, their, their life right. And so we want to have a focus on benefits, we want to drive home benefits constantly. And you're going to see how and where we do that in the flow, and whatnot tomorrow. But right, but the thing to understand is that, and we talked about how a benefit how a feature is a fact about the product or service when we're talking about any part of the product or service for talking about a feature people don't buy products and services for the features they buy products and services for what they believe the features will do for them, the benefit. But sometimes you can have what I call a fo benefit, which is we're still talking about what the prospect will experience, but it's at least one step away from what it is that they really want meaning it's, it's almost an intermediary before the result that the prospect wants. So let me give you an example, let's say we were talking about a supplement for losing weight, and we said that this will flush fat from your intestinal track, right like it'll flush fat out from your intestinal track. That's what you call a fo benefit. It's a fo benefit just like this is a fo benefit, your arteries and blood vessels will instantly relax. Let's say we were selling a supplement for lowering blood pressure. Right. And we said, when you take this right, your arteries and blood vessels will will instantly relax. This is also a an example of a fo benefit. This will make your ads more your Facebook ads more attractive. Why are these fo benefits. These are fo benefits because these are one step removed from what it is that the prospect wants, flushing fat from your intestinal track right if you're if you're interested in in a supplement that that will help you lose weight that will help you lose weight right Flushing fat from your intestinal track. I don't who cares. I don't want I don't care about flushing fat from my intestinal track. Right that's the vehicle by which it works that's how it you know it helps to right to prevent you from absorbing fat from adding fat like blah blah blah blah blah blah. But I don't care about that that's not a benefit to to me right that is that's a fo benefit you're still talking at all three of these instances where we're talking about something that the prospect will experience right that they'll flush fat from their intestinal track that there are will relax that their ads will be more attractive, but it's one step removed from what it is that the prospect cares about right it's one step removed from what it is that the prospect cares about. I want you to be aware of of when you're making when you're stating fo benefits or when you're stating benefits I want you to ask yourself is this what the prospect really wants so the prospect when they were when they were thinking about a weight loss supplement where they thinking I just want to find a supplement that helps flush fat from my intestinal track right or I just want a you know what I care about I want a supplement for my blood right I want a supplement that's going to make my blood vessels and arteries relax right or I want I want something that's going to make my ads more attractive right is that what they really want or is or is what they're after the result the by product of each of these so David said right but don't you need fo benefits to build a bridge from the mechanism to the outcome they want sometimes right sometimes you can use these like you you certainly when when we are explaining let's say it's a great question I love the question when when we are explaining the way that the let's say we've got a supplement to tell people lose fat right to lose weight and part of that is right because of the formulation our formulation. You know our formulation works on three mechanisms in the body three different kind of systems in the body. And one of those things that the supplement does that this formulation does is it helps to flush fat from your intestinal track right and so that's what we call a full benefit or what you could really say another way to think about this is it's an incomplete benefit. It be haven't reached the destination yet right this will flush fat from your intestinal track preventing your body from absorbing fat so that you blah blah blah can eat to your hearts content without gaining any more weight. Now that is the benefit that becomes the complete benefit but the flushing fat from your intestinal track is a full benefit it's incomplete right we haven't gotten to the payoff the real pay off that there is. And so this is something that you need to be aware of because like what David is saying is sometimes you can use these full benefits to as a bridge as a as one step before you get to the the actual pay off even like right your arteries and blood vessels will will instantly relax right so that your heart doesn't need to work as hard experiencing a nice healthy decline in your blood pressure that lasts for hours or something like that that end part is the real benefit that they care about they don't care about the arteries and vessels will instantly relax it's almost you know another way of saying full benefit incomplete benefit is these are really vehicle benefits these are kind of this is how the thing works right but what is the result of making your ads more attractive. Right you'll get you know makes your ads more attractive so that you are so you get more clicks so you get more visitors so you get more prospects into your campaign at a lower cost right allowing you to pay for even more and more traffic that's the benefit of making your ads more attractive right and so simple to take what David said which he is really spot on right you could take these full benefits. Right what what I'm saying here is not that you want to get rid of these is not that you want to eliminate these is that you want to make sure that you go to the end benefit that you give the payoff that you give the result of this the result that the prospect cares about this is incomplete and so you can turn these into complete legit benefits right with simple statements like so you can so you will so you see x y. Which means you right and right so when we say like you know flush fat from your intestinal track so that you so that your body doesn't absorb fat you can eat to your you know so that your body absorbs you know one tenth the amount of fat so you can eat till your hearts content without gaining any more way. This flush is fat from your intestinal track which means that you can eat until your hearts content without having to worry about adding fat right this flush is fat from your intestinal track. And allows you to eat till your you know till your hearts content without ever gaining an ounce of fat see all of these allow you to turn that full benefit into the real benefit. And so it's important that you understand that you understand the that you understand you want to focus on the payoff you want to focus on what the prospect cares about right and so you have to go back you have to look at this understand these. even like this is we're just scratching the surface but for many of you right the reality is is just turning these things into taking them to the next step is powerful in itself because you're focusing on what the prospect cares about instead of focusing on the one step removed or two step removed you know benefits and so let me move this over here and so I want to talk about contrast for one second because this is in the in the context of benefits like and you're going to see all these principles at play when we get into the nitty gritty details like the nitty gritty word choice sentence structure all that like the heart and soul of really the heart and soul is workshop really tomorrow I want you to understand we're going to we're going to be bringing this in when we talk about benefits right you have to understand the power of contrast the power of you know there's contrast needs to be used in copy you know contrast just for definition right this is the like the photographers understand this contrast is the state of being significantly different from something else significantly different from something else you can see the difference in contrast between these two images. So if you can see on your screen the black box and the little circle and then you can see the black box over here and the little circle and you can see the stark difference in contrast between these these two right the strikingly different than something else. It's important that you understand contrast because every solution is made more valuable in the context of the problem it solves right every solution is made more valuable in the context of the problem that it solves and the more severe the problem is the more valuable the more severe the problem is this is why going back to what we talked about earlier about the the buckets of problems and why the the unresolved distress, especially a bleeding neck problem is much easier to sell it's also easier to sell at a higher price point why because solutions are only as valuable as the severity of the problems that they solve the more severe the problem the more valuable the solution is a great example of this is is and I recently talked to my own team about this a great example of this is if we were selling a bandage $1,000 bandage and we were talking to somebody who had a cut on their leg and that cut on their leg they felt what was going on and that was a superficial you know cut was a was a flesh wound, you know if you will. Right and now we have a thousand dollar bandage well the likelihood of us selling them that thousand dollar bandage is really slim to none. If we showed them in being truthful if we showed them that that wasn't a flesh wound that that cut on their leg actually nicked an artery right and that there is a possibility of them bleeding out and the single most efficient most effective way to ensure that they do not bleed out and that they stop the bleeding is with this bandage now it makes that thousand dollar bandage that much more valuable. It makes the price almost seen in in significant when it's presented in the context of this could save your life, but before when that problem was perceived to be a flesh wound, then there was no way in heck that somebody was going to spend a thousand bucks on that on that bandage. And so my point in sharing this with you is that we're we're always, you know, we are presenting contrast, even when we are talking about benefits, especially when we're dealing with somebody that's got an unresolved distress that type of problem. Right, we want to show them the solution and the outcome in context of what it is that they're dealing with in context of the problems that they have in context of the struggle that they have in context of the path that they're on and where it's leading. So we want to contrast the experience that they can have with your solution compared to the experience that they've had and are having up to this point and what they're likely to have if they don't take action and leverage this solution contrast right in your copy is enormously valuable contrast. We want to contrast life in essence, we want to contrast life without the solution, right, like, you know, and this really applies and you're going to see this tomorrow. This really applies much more so to when you're dealing with prospects that have a problem that falls into the unresolved distress bucket, right, you want to show the solution in contrast to the pain to the difficulty to the frustration to the anxiety to the fear to all the negative that they're experiencing right now, because the solution in contrast with that makes the solution that much more valuable right like never again will you feel humiliated when you take your shirt off at the beach instead you'll notice women looking and men admiring as you confidently strut around with a six pack. This might be goofy copy that I just wrote just for this, but you're seeing the right the it's the contrast here right instead of simply just saying right you'll see women looking around men admiring as you confidently strut around with a six pack. You're you're presenting more contrast and that contrast makes the solution pop right just like in an image that contrast can make the things that you want to pop pop when you don't have contrast nothing pops right like it almost becomes flat right and when you want something to pop in an image right when you want it to to stand out when you want it to be significant you add contrast. That's the same thing that we do in copy and contrast in this context is the negative of the existing problem against the positive of the solution. It's the negative of the present compared to the positive of the future. And so really important. And so. Okay, let's go on and I want to cover quickly some terms and definitions I know that I just want to make sure that we're all on the same page because as we get more into some of these things. And especially tomorrow when we get to the tactical, you know nitty gritty stuff and I'm referring back to certain terms. I want to make sure that you guys and gals understand and so the first term that I just want to cover is hook and theme. So when you hear me talk about the hook and theme. Right. What we're really talking about is the idea or angle behind the marketing message. So it's the the idea behind the marketing message it's the idea behind the behind the headline a hook and a headline are two different things. The headline is how you communicate the hook, the theme, the idea. You know, behind every headline is a hook idea theme, it might be ordinary might be plain vanilla, right but the headline and the hook are two different things, right so there's a number of different ways to communicate the same underlying idea. Right so you can have one idea and then you can choose a variety of different words in the headline to communicate that idea. But the hook the theme, the idea, or is the idea behind the marketing message. And what we're looking for is an idea that gets attention that creates interest and I'm going to explain how we do that what are the elements that we we look for and some simple ways to develop the these hooks, but that's the hook in the theme. So I'm not talking about when I talk about hook theme. I'm not talking about the hook or theme for your your product I'm not talking about. Excuse me the big idea for your product like you have a disruptive app and blah blah blah I'm not talking about any of that. I'm talking about the idea behind your marketing message, the idea that you are presenting in your headline, the idea, the narrative, the theme that you are basing your marketing message around. That's what I mean when I say hook and theme. The campaign lead is just the first 300 400 500 words of your marketing message don't get caught up in the exact number. It is the, it's the start of your campaign, whether you are using a video sales letter whether using a webinar whether using a long form sales letter in any piece of communication whether you're giving a speech a presentation what what not in every presentation there is a lead. Right the idea the lead comes from journalism and journalism, you know, maybe you've heard the expression don't bury the lead. The idea of burying the lead is you bury the most exciting part the most important part of a story of an essay in the middle or towards the bottom. They say don't bury the lead right. What they're talking about is like, don't you know don't bury the most valuable, most enticing most compelling part. Start with it lead with it. That's why it's called the campaign lead and the campaign lead serves a very specific role. What you want to do in the opening of your marketing message in the opening of your copy is critical. I'll tell you that that in many, many cases. Look and I don't want to say anything that could seem contradictory to what I said earlier about the offer but 80% of the sales determined by the 80% of response is determined by the lead lead consists of the headline the debt copy the headline complex any whatever if you're using a pre head headline subhead, whatever. And right the first 350 400 500 or so words is critical right it it's make or break it's make or break because ultimately, many people will never even get to your offer if you don't have a good lead the lead determines whether they whether you turn attention into engagement. Right whether you turn attention right it's one thing to get attention. It's another thing to turn that attention into engagement. So right, many of you that have video sales letters when you look at the analytics, and you see that there is a huge drop off in the first 20 seconds, right like where 70% of your visitors are gone. Well, that doesn't mean you didn't get attention that means you didn't translate that attention into engagement. And so the lead the first 350 400 500 or so words is there to create that engagement. I'm going to show you how we, how we do that. The offer, you know, the offer is what does the prospect get and what do they need to give to get it. Now I want to point something out here, and I want you to listen to me, we're going to unpack this in a in a in a little bit and get more in depth in this with this, but I want you to listen to me, please, this is important. Okay, so what most people do not what most marketers don't understand when we say the offer is what is the prospect get, and what do they need to give to get it that we're not just talking about what what do they need to give they need to give a thousand bucks, and what do they get they get dvds and they get videos DVDs shows you my age. They get beta max. They get videos and they get cheat sheets and they get this and they get this and they get this and they get this. No, that's not what we're talking about. We're talking about yes, what do they need to give in terms of dollars and cents of course, right. And what do they get yes in terms of deliverables, but we're also talking about what is the payoff, like what's the result. What's the outcome that they get. What's the change what's the life improvement what's the alleviation of pain or what pain gets alleviated right those are all the things that they they get is there any kind of guarantee that they that they get right like assurance right and then and this is the important part that I want you to understand a big part of the offer in terms of what do they need to give to get it is not just money, but is time is is effort right it on energy. Like what do they need to do. And so that is an important distinction that a lot of marketers completely miss that part of making and we're going to cover this that part of making a truly irresistible proposition is that the perception of what the prospect needs to give compared to what it is that they're going to get and how quickly they're going to get it how easy they're going to get it right like the person that perception, we have to control that right and and you're going to understand what I mean when we get into the details of putting together this one piece that most people neglect, but you have to understand that there's more than just money that there's their time energy like what am I going to have to do am I going to have to be outside for eight hours a day to like like right it's one thing to say, part of this offer, you get a green lush, you know, magnificent lawn that your neighbors will comment about they'll be talking about right you'll feel pride of ownership blah blah blah blah blah right and you get this bag of fertilizer you get this, you know, the spreader and you get this and you get this, but it's another thing to then like have the prospect see like all right I got to give. Great, I only got to give 50 bucks for all this which is great, but then it's going to take me eight hours of my time to do this, it's physical manual labor that I'm going to have to do outside. It's going to take a long time for me to see results. There's a small likelihood of me seeing results right like and so I could put in all this effort and so right i'm giving really i'm giving up kind of risk, if you will, like i'm risking right see there's a lot more that goes into understanding what the prospect needs to give to get the result we have to address that but that's what we're talking about when we say the offer the offer is how we frame all of that the offer is how we present not only the the deliverables and the benefits of those deliverables and the price and the terms and the guarantee right and any kind of premiums but also the how attractive is the solution that they're going to get like how attractive how easy how simple how quick how straight forward is the solution that they're going to get because as you'll understand in just a little bit because right if you've got a crappy solution one that requires a lot of work one that requires a lot of time one one that has a steep learning curve one that has a low likelihood of success. You can put together all the deliverables in the world with a ton of benefits right a great you know great price ridiculous terms right a tremendous guarantee and you're going to get a low volume of takers why because the solution isn't attractive because it requires them to give of their time of their effort of their energy of risk right. And so you're going to understand that but that's the the the offer, then we've got the campaign argument campaign argument is really the body copy. This is where this is where we're actually giving the logical support this is where we're making claims, we're giving proof we're giving evidence we're giving support, and we're ultimately leading to what we call which we're going to talk about the buying belief the one belief that you need your prospects to have before you present your offer. So the campaign argument is the body think of the think of the think of your campaign like this. Let's do it. Let's do this. Let me do this. Do this. Never good when I go into the little. When I go into the little whiteboard pen thingy, but let's do, let's do this I want you to think of your campaign, and you're going to see this. Oops, you're going to see this. Do this with the mouse. You're going to see this in a little bit. This is, this is your marketing campaign. This is the campaign. This is how I break down every campaign follows this simple little timeline. This is selling. This is marketing. This is 75% of the campaign. This is 25% of the of the campaign. This is where we introduce the product. This is where we present your offer. All of this right here is the marketing. This is where we're leading the prospect to want your product or service before we ever even offer to them in here we've got the beginning part. This is the lead over here, right, which makes up the body of the of the campaign of your copy. We've got the campaign argument, and this is where we are ultimately leading your prospect to what we call buying belief, which we're going to talk about in a little bit that sets up the sale for your product or or service. And so when I say the campaign argument, I'm talking about this portion, the body copy of your of your message. And so with that, back here, unique selling proposition. So let's cover this real quick. I've covered this so many times before I'm just going to breeze through this for for those of you that that maybe haven't heard me talk about this before unique selling proposition a USP is a is a term that was first coined by direct response legend, Rossa Reeves, unique selling proposition is the simplest way to think about this because there's the unique selling proposition and there's the unique mechanism needs to are not at all the same thing they're not at all similar. So unique selling proposition is when your product or service offers a unique benefit compared to the other products and services that you are competing with. So when there is a unique benefit when there is a unique promise that you can make a unique benefit that comes with your product or service that you would say that you have a USP. So simply think about the USP as a unique benefit. What benefit do you offer and only you offer that your competitors do not offer, right? What benefit that your prospects care about, right? Has to be what your prospects care about has to be what they want, right? What unique benefit does your product or service offer? Here's the reality. The reality is that today USPs are rare. People still talk about you and what's your unique selling proposition and they've mangled it, right? A real USP is when you offer a benefit that none of your competitors offer. The reality is that today it is rare. Today USPs are typically only associated with disruptive products or services. So I've talked about when Uber first came on the scene, right? They had a USP at the time, right? It was the first time that you could hail a ride with your phone. You didn't need to pay with cash, right? Like at the time you didn't need to tip. You could feel more secure. You didn't need to wait in the taxi lines, right? There was a USP. Today they no longer have a USP thanks to other ride sharing services like Lyft or whatnot. But that was at the time a unique benefit. Same thing with when Netflix went from when they moved away from the DVDs and Blu-rays and went all online. They had a unique selling proposition, right? Where you didn't need to leave your home and you could rent a movie. There was no need for you to return the movie so there were no late fees. And then it became right. You had an endless library of videos to choose from. You could play a movie in seconds, one flat fee, blah, blah, blah, blah. Today, of course, we know that none of those things are a unique selling proposition anymore. Again, a USP is rare. When you do have one, great, cool. Use it. We're going to use it in the copy. We're going to highlight it, right? But the reality is that for most of us, having a USP is going to be unlikely. And so how do we differentiate then if we don't have a unique benefit? In other words, we still have to differentiate. You heard me say earlier that what people care about, what gets attention is different, right? People want to know what's different. They're not looking for more of the same. They want to know what is different. What's different about what you're talking about? What's different about the way that your thing works? What's different about the way that your product produces the results? Right? They don't want more of the same. They want different. And so, right, if we don't have a different benefit or outcome or result that we can claim for our product or service, because we don't have a USP, how do we differentiate? We differentiate with a unique mechanism, a unique mechanism. And we're going to talk about this ever so slightly because I talk about this all the time, all over the place that you guys can find. But unique mechanism is really how your product or service works. So the unique mechanism is the unique process, formula, component, part, stage, or stages, you know, approach, system behind your product or service. The way to think about it is the unique mechanism is how does your product or service work to produce the result, right? And we're saying uniquely, how does it uniquely work? What's different about the way that your product or service works to produce the result, the outcome, the promise, the payoff compared to every other method out there? So the unique mechanism, the unique selling proposition is a unique benefit. The unique mechanism does not require a unique benefit. It allows you to differentiate by taking the same benefit others are promising, but it allows you to say, I have a different way of giving you that, and that different way is superior to all of the other ways. That's the unique mechanism. It is how your product or service works. So right now to just start to think about this for a second, and we're going to unpack this a little bit in just a bit, is that like you claim that your product or service produces a result. How? Why? Why is it able to produce that result? What does it do? How does it work to produce that result? I'm not asking you right now to like give me copy. I'm not asking you to, I'm asking you, I'm saying that as a way for you to begin to think about that, right? How does your product or service, if you have a training approach to help people build muscle, how does it work? Why does your training approach work? What is it about your training approach that causes it to build muscle for people so efficiently, so effectively, so effectively in such a short period of time? Right? And so how does it work? If you've got a supplement that helps to reduce cholesterol, well, how does it work? What is it about the supplement? Is it one nutrient? Is it a combination of nutrients? Is it the dosage in there? Is it the combination of the format, the type of nutrients in there? Is it the way that it is encapsulated so that it goes through digestion, your digestive process? Right? Like what is it about the way that your product or service works to produce the result? That is extremely important. And we're going to get into that, understanding that in just a little bit because this is going to become the foundation of what we do. Right? All the tactical stuff that we're going to talk about tomorrow, all the word choice, right? All the sentence structure, all the flow, all the gambits, all the moves, all the framing, right? It's done with a purpose, right, based on this foundational stuff, right? And so that's why I want you to understand this. And then we've got the catalyst. And catalyst, you're going to hear me, you're not going to hear me talk about all that much. But the way that I want you to think of the catalyst is, I want you to think of a catalyst as something that is happening or has recently happened or is about to happen that has either opened up a new opportunity or has made it so that the mechanism can work to produce the result. So let me say that again. So catalyst is something that has either recently happened, it is happening now, or it is about to happen that opens up a new opportunity. And or is the reason why the unique mechanism is so appropriate, so effective right now, right? And so a catalyst, perfect example of a catalyst, right, is the, the, when I said earlier, not a perfect example, but an example of a catalyst is the change in the change in the IOS tracking, right? That is a catalyst that has created, like, like there are many promotions happening right now in the world of tracking in the world of reporting in the world of pixels in the world of media buying that is based on this catalyst of, right, there's now this change, Apple made this change, that change has caused XYZ disruption, and because of that, you now need Element OP. That's a catalyst. Another example of a catalyst is like when in the world of let's say financial investing, when a catalyst might be such and such company just announced an upcoming IPO on, you know, whatever, November 1 or something like that, right? That's an occurrence, that's something that is about to happen, that creates an opportunity. That opportunity in this little example is to get in on potentially an IPO or, right, we might link that catalyst to lots of companies now are going to, you know, IPO because of blah, blah, blah, could be a law being changed, right? That could be in the world of alternative health, there could be the legality could be, right, this, you know, this company is now about to release this new supplement, that, right, and thanks to this change in a law that now legalize this particular, you know, supplement or whatnot, that change in the law is the catalyst behind this new opportunity. In this context when I say new opportunity, I mean the opportunity to get this supplement that at one point you couldn't get. Now you could also have a catalyst just to show you and not all the time like the catalyst Scott said, a window of opportunity, that's correct, that's a great way to look at it. It's something, something is happening or something is about to happen or something very recently happened that has created a window of opportunity, a window of opportunity to take advantage of something or something has happened or is happening right now or is about to happen that is creating a new need that didn't exist before. So when people were talking about the coming, the impending iOS update, that's a catalyst that was about to happen, right, and talking about the you got to make changes to your tracking, if not, once this thing goes into effect on XYZ date, you're, you're dead in the water with your, your tracking, that's, that's another example of a, of a catalyst, sometimes a catalyst exists, like sometimes we can find or spot a catalyst, not all the time. And so I'm going to see that there are, there are many things that we that we're going to talk about, there are many techniques and tactics and strategies that don't apply in every situation right copy isn't like I said at the very beginning there's not this one size fits all every circumstance is every single thing applies to every single product or service in every single circumstance, it does not work that way, but I'm sharing this with you because you're going to hear me talk about leveraging a catalyst, a catalyst is a great way, especially, especially when the catalyst is about to happen, or recently happened, and it's about to cause damage catalyst is a great way to create what's called engagement urgency engagement urgency is really another way of saying it is, is is when you give your prospect the reason to engage with your marketing now, meaning you prevent them from procrastinating on engaging with your marketing, right, how many times have you have you maybe maybe I'm the only one, but how many times have you come across a marketing piece for something that you are potentially interested in, but there was no reason for you to stop exactly what you were, you know, stop what you were doing and engage with that marketing piece right now. And so you saved it for later you put it into your to do list manager, you just saved it as a bookmark or whatnot for later on and then you forgot about it you never went back to it. Right and so there are steps that we can take to create these in, you know, to create this engagement urgency to give prospects a reason to engage now, I'm not saying this is different from offer urgency which is giving them a reason to respond to the offer now, I'm talking about giving them a reason to engage with the marketing now, you've got to recognize, and this is an important thing for you guys to just file away that you got to recognize that, you know, like, don't ever underestimate the task at hand, don't think that just because somebody clicks on your ad and comes to your page that now, they're going to engage with your, your marketing, you don't know how many clients, how many students I've had come into and they say like, I, you know, the amount we get call it 1000 clicks to our VSL page, and we've got, you know, 680 people engaging with the video, right, meaning, you know, whatever 350 people out of you know 35% don't even engage with the video, they don't even press play on the video, let alone consume the whole video and whatnot and so we can't take these things for granted and so think of a catalyst something that is going on right now something that recently happened or something that is about to happen as an opportunity to create that that engagement urgency as well it is a it gives justification in the mind of the prospect for why now is the time to act on this right why now is the time to engage in why now is the time to act on this because of this catalyst when you have things like for example when you have things like there's a, you know, like a law is about to to to pass that's gonna you know that's gonna outlaw XYZ nutrient and so now is the time for you to get this before it's illegal right understand like that's extremely powerful because that gives a justification for why they need to act now, it also gives a justification for the fear of missing out right there's a reason behind it and so a catalyst can be used in that way. And so let me see here and so with that, we're going to stop and we are going to take a break for lunch but before we do, let me just go through and see if there are any questions. So, Edward says what exactly are the differences between faux benefits and functional benefits. So, not every so the way to think about this and it's a great question that I love the way that your, your mind works with this. So, I would say that that they're both benefits they're both talking about something that the prospect wants, but they are the functional benefit is is something that the prospect wants. It is something that they care about, but it's just not dimensionalized, whereas a faux benefit is talking about something that the prospect will experience, but it's not something that they that they really that they care about that they talk about that's top of mind right. It's, it's likely in the in the mechanical pencil example that somebody wants, you know, like I'm a writer man I want a pencil that's, it's easy to, you know, easy to hold that's comfortable to hold right. That's the that's the difference hopefully that makes sense. Think of again the full benefits as it's it on the surface it seems like a benefit because we're talking about something the prospect is going to experience but it's not really what they care about.
